attributes.stem:
A 70-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with intense right calf pain and swelling four days after an elective right total hip arthroplasty. He denies chest pain, shortness of breath, recent falls, or trauma. His medical history includes atrial fibrillation, for which he takes warfarin. This was temporarily ceased prior to the operation and has since been recommenced. He has been receiving heparin as bridging therapy while his INR remains sub-therapeutic.

On examination, he appears uncomfortable. His pulse rate is 88/min, blood pressure 130/80 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. There is right calf swelling without pitting oedema.

Investigations reveal:

  • Haemoglobin 130 g/L (115-155)

  • Platelets 30 x109/L (150-450)

  • White cell count 10.2 x109/L (4.0-10.0)

  • INR 1.8 (0.9-1.2)

  • aPTT 40 seconds (24-38)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's presentation?
attributes.explanation:
This patient's presentation is most consistent with heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT), which is a drug-related adverse effect of heparin therapy. HIT typically occurs 5-10 days after starting heparin and is characterized by a significant drop in platelet count (>50% from baseline) and paradoxical thrombotic complications. In this case, the patient has developed thrombocytopenia (platelets 30 x x109/L) and likely has a deep vein thrombosis (DVT) in his right leg, manifesting as calf pain and swelling.

A peri-prosthetic haematoma may cause pain and swelling in the leg; however, it does not explain the severe thrombocytopenia or the timing of symptom onset (four days post-surgery).

While the patient's INR is sub-therapeutic, this is unlikely to be the primary cause of his presentation. He has been receiving heparin as bridging therapy, which should provide adequate anticoagulation. Moreover, sub-therapeutic INR alone does not explain the severe thrombocytopenia.

Fat embolism syndrome is a potential complication of long bone fractures or orthopaedic procedures. However, it typically presents with respiratory distress, neurological symptoms, and a petechial rash, none of which are present in this case. Additionally, fat embolism does not cause severe thrombocytopenia.

Iatrogenic nerve injury is unlikely to present four days after surgery and does not explain the patient's leg swelling or thrombocytopenia.

The diagnosis of HIT is supported by the timing of symptom onset, the severe thrombocytopenia, and the thrombotic complication (likely DVT) in the context of recent surgery and heparin exposure. The next steps in management would include discontinuing all heparin products, initiating an alternative anticoagulant (such as argatroban or fondaparinux), and confirming the diagnosis with HIT antibody testing and functional assays.
learningPoints: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) can predispose patients to vascular thrombosis, particularly in the context of recent surgery.
HIT typically occurs 5-10 days after starting heparin therapy and is characterized by a significant drop in platelet count and thrombotic complications.
The 4T score is a clinical tool used to assess the probability of HIT in patients receiving heparin therapy.
references: Ahmed I, Majeed A, Powell R. Heparin induced thrombocytopenia: diagnosis and management update. Postgrad Med J. 2007;83(983):575-582. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2600013/

Warkentin TE, Levine MN, Hirsh J, Horsewood P, Roberts RS, Gent M, Kelton JG Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia in patients treated with low-molecular-weight heparin or unfractionated heparin. N Engl J Med. 1995 May 18;332(20):1330-5. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/7715641/
